question: At what temperature is the root mean square speed of an atom in an argon gas cylinder equal to the rms speed of a helium gas atom at $ - 20^\circ C$ ? (Atomic mass of  $Ar = 39.9\,\,u\,\,of{\text{ }}He = 4.0\,\,u)$
explanation:
Root mean square speed of argon atom
${\left( {{v_{rms}}} \right)_{Ar}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3R{T_{Ar}}}}{{{M_{Ar}}}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Root mean square speed of helium atom
${\left( {{v_{rms}}} \right)_{He}} = \sqrt {\frac{{3R{T_{He}}}}{{{M_{He}}}}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2)
$\dfrac{\left(v_{r m s}\right)_{A r}}{\left(v_{r m s}\right)_{H e}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 R T_{A r}}{M_{A r}} \times \dfrac{M_{H e}}{3 R T_{H e}}}$
$\begin{gathered}\text { Given }\left(v_{r m s}\right)_{A r}=\left(v_{r m s}\right)_{H e} \\\Rightarrow 1=\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{T_{A r}}{T_{H e}}\right)\left(\dfrac{M_{H e}}{M_{A r}}\right)} \\\Rightarrow \dfrac{M_{H e}}{M_{A r}}=\dfrac{T_{H e}}{T_{A r}} \\T_{A r}=253.15\left(\dfrac{39.9}{4}\right)=2.52 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~K} .\end{gathered}$

question:
The mass of hydrogen molecules is
$3.32 \times {10^{ - 27}} kg$. If $10^{23}$ hydrogen molecules}
strike per second at on $2\,\,c{m^2}$ area of a rigid wall at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ from the normal and are rebound back with speed of $1000\,\,m/s$, then the pressure exerted on the wall is (Assume collision is elastic)
explanation:
Given that the collision is elastic. The component of the linear momentum along the surface remains same and the perpendicular component gets reversed.
$\Delta P=2 m v_{N}$
Where $v_{N}$ is the compound of velocity along normal direction. The pressure acting on the wall is $P=\dfrac{F}{A}=\dfrac{2 N m V_{N}}{A}$ (N. number of molecules striking per second)
$\begin{aligned}P & =\dfrac{2 \times 10^{23} \times 3.32 \times 10^{-27} \times 1000}{\sqrt{2} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}} \\& =2.34 \times 10^{3} \text { Pascal }\end{aligned}$

question: A spherical balloon of volume $V$ contains helium at pressure P. How many moles of helium are in the balloon if the average kinetic energy of the helium atoms is $\bar{K}$ ?

$PV = \frac{2}{3}N\bar K$
$N = \frac{3}{2}\frac{{PV}}{{\bar K}}$
$n = \frac{N}{{{N_A}}} = \frac{3}{2}\frac{{PV}}{{\bar K{N_A}}}$

question: The molecules in an ideal gas at $27^\circ C$ have a certain mean velocity. At what approximate temperature, will the mean velocity be doubled
explanation:
Mean velocity of gas molecules is, $v=\sqrt{\dfrac{8 R T}{\pi M}}$ where, $T = $ Temperature of the gas in kelvin $M = $ Molar mass,
$R = $ Universal gas constant
For the same gas, $v \propto \sqrt{T}$
$\therefore \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{300}{T_{2}}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{1}}{2 v_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{300}{T_{2}}}$
Squaring both sides, we get, $\dfrac{1}{4}=\dfrac{300}{T_{2}}$
${T_2} = 1200\,\,K = 1200 - 273 = 927^\circ C$

question: Match the following statements associated with the Equipartition of Energy in Gases and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A corresponds to Q because the kinetic energy equation for a molecule involves the square of its velocity components.
B corresponds to R as each degree of freedom, whether translational or rotational, contributes $\dfrac{1}{2} k_{B} T$ to the total energy in thermal equilibrium.
C corresponds to S because monatomic gases such as helium or argon have only translational degrees of freedom.
D corresponds to P since in thermal equilibrium, all degrees of freedom have the same average energy. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - S,  B - Q, C - R,      D - P
A - P,  B - R,     C - S,  D - Q
A - Q,  B - S,  C - P,  D - R

question: Match the following terms related to the Law of Equipartition of Energy and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A corresponds to Q because the average energy for a molecule with three translational degrees of freedom (as in a monatomic gas) is $\dfrac{3}{2} k_{B} T$.
B corresponds to R since the equipartition theorem states that energy is equally distributed among all accessible degrees of freedom.
C corresponds to S because the average kinetic energy per degree of freedom at temperature T is $\dfrac{1}{2} k_{B} T$.
D corresponds to P because a molecule moving in space has three degrees of freedom and requires three coordinates to specify its location. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - P, B - Q, C - R,  D - S
A - Q,  B - R,    C - S,  D - P
A - S,  B - P,  C - Q,  D - R
A - Q,   B - R,  C - S,  D - P

explanation:

question: A gas mixture consists of 8 moles of argon and 6 moles of oxygen at temperature ${T}$. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
explanation:
Number of moles of argon, ${n_{1}=8}$
Number of moles of oxygen, ${n_{2}=6}$
For an ideal gas, internal energy, ${U=\dfrac{f}{2} n R T}$ where ' ${f}$ ' is degree of freedom
${\therefore U_{1}=\dfrac{f_{1}}{2} n_{1} R T}$ and ${U_{2}=\dfrac{f_{2}}{2} n_{2} R T}$
Total energy, ${U=U_{1}+U_{2}}$
${U=\dfrac{f_{1}}{2} n_{1} R T+\dfrac{f_{2}}{2} n_{2} R T}$
${U=\dfrac{3}{2} \times 8 R T+\dfrac{5}{2} \times 6 R T=27 R T}$
So, correct option is (3).

question: The temperature of a gas is ${-78^{\circ} {C}}$ and the average translational kinetic energy of its molecules is ${K}$. The temperature at which the average translational kinetic energy of the molecules of the same gas becomes ${2 K}$ is

As we know that, $K.E. \propto T,$ when kinetic energy is doubled, temperature also double.
${K_{f}=2 k \Rightarrow T_{f}=2 T}$
${T_{f}=2 \times 195=390 {~K}=117^{\circ} {C}}$
So, correct option is (3).

question: The ratio of translational and rational kinetic energy at 100 $K$ temperature is 3:2. Find the internal energy of one mole gas at this temperature

$ \Rightarrow \quad 6 = 3f - 9 \Rightarrow f = 5$
Now $U=\dfrac{f}{2} n R T=\dfrac{5}{2} \times 1 \times 8.3 \times 100=2075 {~J}$

question:
A mixture of two gases consists of $3$ moles of nitrogen and $6$ moles of neon at temperature $27^\circ C$. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
$\left( {R = 8.32\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\,{K^{ - 1}}} \right)$
explanation:
Internal energy of a gas with $f$ degrees of freedom,
$U = \frac{f}{2}nRT$
Now, ${f_{{N_2}}} = \frac{5}{2},{f_{Ne}} = \frac{3}{2}$
$\therefore \,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} {U_{total{\rm{ }}}} = {U_{{N_2}}} + {U_{Ne}}$
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} = \frac{5}{2} \times 3 \times 8.32 \times (27 + 273) + $
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \frac{3}{2} \times 6 \times 8.32 \times (27 + 273)$
$\,\,\,\, = 3 \times 8.32 \times 300 \times \left( {\frac{5}{2} + 3} \right)$
$ = 4.16 \times 900 \times 11$
$ = 41184\,J$
$\therefore {U_{total{\rm{ }}}} = 4.12 \times {10^4}\,J$

$T=3200 K$

question:
Assertion: Vibrational energy of diatomic molecule corresponding to each degree of freedom is $K_{B} T$.

Reason: For every molecule, vibrational degree of freedom is 2 .
explanation:
According to law of equipartition energy in thermal equilibrium, at temperature $T$. each degree of freedom of contributes an average energy equal to $\dfrac{1}{2} K_{B} T$.
The vibrational motion has two types of energy associated with the vibrations along the length of the molecule-kinetic energy and potential energy. Thus it contributes two degrees of freedom. Thus vibration energy
$=2 \times \dfrac{1}{2} K_{B} T=K_{B} T$
So correct option is (3).

Reason: The molecules of a gas collide with each other and the velocities of the molecules change due to the collision.
explanation:
We know $n R=N K_{B}$
$\left(E_{K}\right)_{\text {total }}=\dfrac{3}{2} N K_{B} T=\dfrac{3}{2} n R T$
$=\dfrac{3}{2} P V=1.5 \mathrm{PV}$
Reason is separate fact
So correct option is (2).

question:

Reason: The kinetic energy of ordered mechanical motion is converted into the kinetic energy of random motion of gas molecules.
explanation: The motion of the container is known as the ordered motion of the gas and zig-zag motion of gas molecules within the container is called disordered/random motion, like brownian motion. When the container suddenly stops, ordered kinetic energy gets converted into disordered kinetic energy which increases the temperature of the gas.

question: A light container having a diatomic gas enclosed with in is moving with velocity $v$. Mass of the gas is  $M$ and number of moles is  $n$. The kinetic energy of the gas w.r.to ground is
explanation:
The kinetic energy of gas w.r.t. centre of mass of the system is the kinetic energy of molecules due to their random motion. This will be the internal energy of the gas.
$\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} .=\dfrac{5}{2} n R T$
Given that
Mass of gas $ = M$, Temperature $ = T$
Kinetic energy of gas w.r.t. ground $=$ Kinetic energy of centre of mass w.r.t. ground + Kinetic energy of gas w.r.t. centre of mass.
$K . E=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\dfrac{5}{2} n R T$

question: Match the following terms related to the mean free path and molecular motion and choose the correct option from the codes given below:
explanation:
A corresponds to Q because the mean free path is the average distance a molecule travels before colliding with another molecule, which is approximately $2.9 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,m$ for air molecules at standard temperature and pressure $(STP)$.
B corresponds to R as the collision rate is determined by the product of the number density of molecules ( $n$ ), the cross-sectional area ( $\pi d^{2}$ ), and the average speed of the molecules ( $\langle v\rangle$ ).
C corresponds to S since the molecular diameter $(d)$ for air molecules is given as $2 \times {10^{ - 10}}\,m$.
D corresponds to P because the diffusion of gas in a room, such as from a leaking cylinder, is a process that is significantly affected by the mean free path and molecular speed, leading to the observed slow diffusion rate. Correct option is (1).

options.text: A - Q,  B - R,  C - S,   D - P
A - P,  B - S,  C - Q,  D - R
A - R,  B - P,  C - S,  D - Q
A - S,   B - Q,    C - P,  D - R

question: The mean free path for a gas at temperature $300\,K$ and pressure 600 torr is $10^{-7} {~m}$. The mean free path of the gas at a temperature $400 K$ and pressure 200 torr will be
explanation:
As we know that,
$\lambda=\dfrac{K T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} \cdot p}$
$\therefore \quad \lambda \propto \dfrac{T}{p}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}} \times \dfrac{p_{2}}{p_{1}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{10^{-7}}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{300}{400} \times \dfrac{200}{600}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda_{2}=10^{-7} \times \dfrac{4 \times 6}{3 \times 2}=4 \times 10^{-7} {~m}$
subject: Physics
topic: Mean Free Path and Mean Time between Two Successive Collisions
subtopic: Mean Free Path and Mean Time between Two Successive Collisions
parent_topic: Mean Free Path and Mean Time between Two Successive Collisions

question: Mean free path of the molecules in a polyatomic gas is independent of
explanation:
Mean free path
$\lambda=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} n_{v}}=\dfrac{v t}{\sqrt{2}\left(\pi d^{2} v t\right) n_{v}}=\dfrac{v t}{\sqrt{2}(V) n_{v}}$
So clearly, mean free path depends on number density of the molecules, volume of the molecule, temperature of the gas (as average velocity of gas molecules depend on temperature of the gas).
But mean free path doesn't depend on gas constant $R$.

question: Consider an ideal gas at pressure $p$, volume $V$ and temperature $T$. The mean free path for molecules of the gas is $L$. If the radius of gas molecules, as well as pressure, volume and temperature of the gas are doubled, then the mean free path will be
explanation:
As,
Mean free path,
$\lambda=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} R T}{\pi r^{2} N p}$
$\therefore \quad L = \frac{{2\sqrt 2 RT}}{{\pi {r^2}Np}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
According to question if the radius of gas molecules, as well as pressure, volume and temperature of gas are doubled then
$\lambda^{\prime}=\dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} R \cdot 2 T}{\pi \times 4 r^{2} \times N \times 2 p}$
$=\dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{2 \sqrt{2} R T}{\pi r^{2} N p}=\dfrac{L}{4}$ (From Eq. (1))
Hence, the mean free path will be $L / 4$.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct options give below.
The mean free path of gas molecules is inversely proportional to square of molecular diameter.
Average kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to absolute temperature of gas.
explanation:
Average ${K . E}$. of gas molecules ${=\dfrac{f}{2} k T}$
where, ${f}$ is the degree of freedom of molecule.
${\therefore(K . E) \propto T}$
Also, mean free path of gas molecule
${(\lambda)=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} n}}$
${\therefore \lambda \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}}$
Hence both statement ${A}$ and ${B}$ are correct.
So, correct option is (3).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A incorrect but Statement B is correct

question: If the collision frequency of hydrogen molecules in a closed chamber at ${27^{\circ} {C}}$ is ${Z}$, then the collision frequency of the same system at ${127^{\circ} {C}}$ is
explanation:
Collision time between successive collisions is ${\tau \alpha \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}}$ and ${Z=\dfrac{1}{\tau}}$
$\therefore Z \propto \sqrt T \quad \quad (1)$
Here, ${Z_{1}=Z, T_{1}=300 K, Z_{2}=}$ ? and ${T_{2}=400 {~K}}$
From eq. (1), we can write
$\dfrac{Z_{2}}{Z_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}}$
or ${\dfrac{Z_{2}}{Z_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{400}{300}}}$ or ${\dfrac{Z_{2}}{Z_{1}}=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}}$ or
${\Rightarrow Z_{2}=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} Z}$
So, option (4) is correct.

question: In a vessel containing an ideal gas the pressure is $1.1 \times {10^5} Pa$, temperature is $27^\circ C$ & diameter of molecules is $0.8 \,nm$. Find the mean free path of the molecules if Boltzman constant is $1.38 \times {10^{ - 23}} J/K$
explanation:
${\lambda _{mean{\text{ }}}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}\left( {\frac{N}{V}} \right)}}$
$and,{\text{ }}\quad PV = N{K_B}T$
$\therefore {\lambda _{mean{\text{ }}}} = \frac{{{K_B}T}}{{\sqrt 2 \pi {d^2}P}}$
$ = \frac{{1.38 \times {{10}^{ - 23}} \times 300}}{{\sqrt 2 \times 3.14 \times 0.64 \times {{10}^{ - 18}} \times 1.1 \times {{10}^5}}}$
$ = 132 \times {10^{ - 10}}m = 13.2\,\,nm$

question: An ideal gas is enclosed in a cylinder at pressure of $2 \,atm$  and temperature, $300\, K$. The mean time between two successive collisions is $6 \times {10^{ - 8}} s$. If the pressure is doubled and temperature is increased to $500 \,K$, the mean time between two successive collisions will be close to
explanation:
Mean time $(\tau)$ between two successive collisions
$ \Rightarrow \tau \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt T }}\frac{T}{P} = \frac{{\sqrt T }}{P}\therefore \frac{{{\tau _1}}}{{{\tau _2}}} = \frac{{{P_2}}}{{{P_1}}}\sqrt {\frac{{{T_1}}}{{\,{T_2}}}} $
$ \Rightarrow {\tau _2} = \frac{{6 \times {{10}^{ - 8}}}}{2}\sqrt {\frac{{500}}{{300}}} $
$ = \sqrt {15} \times {10^{ - 8}} \approx 4 \times {10^{ - 8}} s$

question: Modern vaccum pumps can evacuate a vessel down to a pressure of $4.0 \times {10^{ - 15}} atm$. At room temperature (300 K). Taking $R = 8.0\,J{\rm{ }}{K^{ - 1}} mol{e^{ - 1}}$, $1 atm = {10^5} Pa$  and ${N_{Avogadro{\rm{ }}}} = 6 \times {10^{23}} mol{e^{ - 1}}$, the mean distance between molecules of gas in an evacuated vessel will be of the order of:
explanation:
From the given information we calculate the average volume occupied by each molecule (not the volume of molecule). The average volume occupied or share of each molecule is equal to the ratio of total volume divides by total number of molecules. This volume can be approximated as a cube of side length $(\lambda)$. The cube root of the average volume per molecule is equal to $(\lambda)$. Here the value $(\lambda)$ is also called as mean free path and it is equal to average distance between two molecules of a gas as shown in the figure.
https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2023_09_19_1c5b4a5eaf0ba03e77ddg-6.jpg?height=205&width=289&top_left_y=777&top_left_x=1266
Ideal gas equation is
$P V=n R T=\dfrac{N}{N_{A}} R T$
Given that
$R = 8\frac{J}{{{\rm{ }}K.mole{\rm{ }}}},T = 300 K$
${N_A} = 6 \times {10^{23}} mol{e^{ - 1}}$
$P = 4.0 \times {10^{ - 15}} \times {10^5}\,N/{m^2}$
$\frac{V}{N} = \frac{{RT}}{{P{N_A}}} = \frac{{8 \times 300}}{{4.0 \times {{10}^{ - 15}} \times {{10}^5} \times 6 \times {{10}^{23}}}}$
${\lambda ^3} = \frac{V}{N} = {10^{ - 11}} {m^3}$
$\lambda = 2.15 \times {10^{ - 4}} = 0.215\,\,mm$

question:
Assertion: Mean free path of a gas molecule varies inversely as density of the gas.


Reason: Mean free path varies inversely as temperature of the gas.
explanation:
The mean free path of a gas molecule is the average distance between two successive collisions. It is represented by $\lambda$.
$\lambda=\dfrac{k_{B} T}{\sqrt{2} \pi \sigma^{2} \rho}$
Here, $\sigma=$ diameter of molecule $k_{B}=$ Boltzmann's constant
Hence, mean free path varies inversely as density of the gas. The mean free path varies directly as the temperature and inversely as the pressure of the gas. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question: If \(\lambda\) represents mean free path of a gas particle pressure \[P\], absolute temperature \[T\] and density \(\rho\) of an ideal gas then predict the correct graph.
explanation:
The mean free path is given by
$\lambda=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi n d^{2}}$
$n$ - number of particles per unit volume
$d$ - diameter of a particle
$\begin{aligned}& n=\dfrac{N}{V}=\dfrac{P N_{A}}{R T}=\dfrac{P}{K_{B} T} \\& \lambda=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \dfrac{K_{B} T}{\pi d^{2} P}\end{aligned}$
So the graph between $\lambda {\rm{ }}\,\& \,\,T$ is a straight line passing through origin. Graph between $\lambda {\rm{ }}\,\& \,\,P$ is hyperbolic.
$\lambda=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi n d^{2}}=\dfrac{m_{0}}{\sqrt{2} \pi\left(m_{0} n\right) d^{2}}=\dfrac{m_{0}}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} \rho}$
Where $m_{0}$ - mass of a gas particle
$m_{0} n$ - Total mass per unit volume
$\left(m_{0} n=\rho\right)$
$\lambda \,\,\alpha \,\,\frac{1}{\rho }$ The graph between $\lambda {\rm{ }}\,\& \,\,\rho $ is hyperbolic.
So option (4) is correct.

question: An ideal gas enclosed in a cylinder at pressure of $1\,atm$ and temperature, $300 K$. The mean time between two successive collisions is $2 \times {10^{ - 8}} s$. If the pressure is doubled and temperature is increased to $400 K$, the mean time between two successive collisions will be close to :
explanation:
The mean time between two successive collisions is $t=\dfrac{\lambda}{v}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} n v}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { where } \quad n_{v}=\dfrac{n N_{A}}{v}=\dfrac{n N_{A}}{\dfrac{n R T}{P}}=\dfrac{N_{A} P}{R T} \\& \Rightarrow \quad t=\dfrac{R T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} N_{A} P v} \\& \Rightarrow \quad t \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{T}}{P} \quad(\text { velocity } v \propto \sqrt{T}) \\& \dfrac{t_{1}}{t_{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{2}}} \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{300}{400}} \times \dfrac{2}{1}=\sqrt{3} \\& \Rightarrow \quad t_{2}=\dfrac{t_{1}}{\sqrt{3}}=1.15 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

question:
Statement I : Gas thermometers are less sensitive than liquid thermometers.
Statement II : The ratio of universal gas constant and a vogadro's number is called Boltzmann's constant.
Statement III : The density of a given mass of a gas at constant pressure is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature.
The correct option among the following is
explanation:
Statement I is false because gas thermometers are more sensitive than liquid thermometers. This is because gas expands more than a liquid.
Statement II is true because $R / N_{A}=k_{B}$
Statement III is true because
Density $=\dfrac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}$
$d = m/V{\rm{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Now, by ideal gas equation
$p V=n R T$
$p V=\dfrac{m}{M} R T$,
$\therefore \quad m=\dfrac{p V M}{R T}$
Put $m=\dfrac{p V M}{R T}$ in Eq. (1)
$d=\dfrac{p V M}{R T V}$
$\Rightarrow d=\dfrac{p M}{R T} \Rightarrow d \propto \dfrac{1}{T}$

options.text: Statements I, II, III are true
Statements I, II are true, but statement III is false
Statements II, III are true, but statement I is false
Statements I, II, III are false

question: The core of the sun is a plasma. It is at a temperature of The order of $10^{7} {~K}$ and contains equal number of protons the electrons. The density of the core of the Sun is $10^{5} {~kg} {~m}^{-3}$ Assume that molar mass of proton is $1 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}$ and the mass of an electron to be negligible compared to the mass of a proton. Estimate the pressure  at the core of the sun. Assume that plasma behaves like an ideal gas.
explanation:
$1 {~m}^{3}$ volume of matter has a mass of $10^{5} {~kg}$ and is mostly due to protons.
$n p=$ number of moles of proton in $1 {~m}^{3}$ volume.$=\dfrac{\text { Mass of protons }}{\text { Molar mass of proton }}=\dfrac{10^{5}}{10^{-3}}=10^{8} {~mol}$
The matter has equal number of electrons. Hence total count of particles is
$2{n_P} = 2 \times {10^8}\,{\rm{mol}}$
$\therefore \quad P = \frac{{nRT}}{V} = \frac{{2 \times {{10}^8} \times 8.3 \times {{10}^7}}}{1}$
$ = 1.66 \times {10^{16}}\,Pa$

question:
A container fitted with a piston, carrying $1.4\,kg$ nitrogen gas, is moving at a constant speed of $57.6\,km{\rm{/}}hr.$ The change in temperature of the nitrogen gas when the container suddenly stops is
(Take $R = 8\,J\,mo{l^{ - 1}}\,{K^{ - 1}},$Molecular weight of ${N_2} = 28\,g$)
[Ignore the energy loss due to friction]
explanation:
For diatomic gas like nitrogen,
$\Delta U = \frac{5}{2}nR\Delta T$
When the container suddenly stops, the $K.E.$ of mechanical motion is converted into internal energy of the gas.
$\therefore \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{5}{2}nR\Delta T$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( \begin{gathered} \because \Delta {\text{V}} = 0 \\ \therefore \Delta {\text{Q}} = \Delta {\text{U}} \\ \end{gathered} \right)$
$\therefore \quad \Delta T = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{{5nR}}$
but $\frac{m}{n} = M$
$\therefore \Delta T = \frac{{M{v^2}}}{{5R}}$
Given, $v = 57.6\,km/hr = 16\,m/s$
$\therefore \Delta T = \frac{{28 \times {{(16)}^2} \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{5 \times 8}}$
$ = 5.6 \times 16 \times 2 \times {10^{ - 3}}$
$ = 0.1792\,K \approx 0.18\,K$
As, $\Delta T$ is equivalent for both the units $^\circ C$ and $K,{\mkern 1mu} $
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \Delta T = 0.18^\circ C$

question: A thermally insulated vessel contains an ideal gas of molecular mass $M$ and ratio of specific heats $\gamma$. It is moving with speed $v$ and it's suddenly brought to rest. Assuming no heat is lost to the surrounding, its temperature increases by
explanation:
Here, work done is zero.
So, loss in kinetic energy $=$ Change in internal energy of gas
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = n{C_V}\Delta T = n\frac{R}{{\gamma - 1}}\Delta T$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{m}{M}\frac{R}{{\gamma - 1}}\Delta T$
$\therefore \Delta T = \frac{{M{v^2}(\gamma - 1)}}{{2R}}K\quad (K - {\rm{ }}Kelvin{\rm{ }})$

question: If $V_{0}$ is the volume of a standard unit cell of germanium crystal containing $N_{0}$ atoms, then the expression for the mass $m$ of a volume $V$ in terms of $V_{0}, N_{0}, M_{\text {mol }}$ and $N_{A}$ is [here, $M_{\text {mole }}$ is the molar mass of germanium and $N_{A}$ is the Avogadro's constant]
explanation:
Number of unit cells in volume $V$
$=\dfrac{\text { Total volume }}{\text { Volume of a unit cell }}=\dfrac{V}{V_{0}}$
Number of atoms in volume $V$
$=$ Number of unit cells $\times$ Number of atoms in1 unit cell $=\dfrac{V}{V_{0}} \times N_{0}$
Number of moles in volume $V$
$=\dfrac{\text { Number of atoms }}{\text { Avogadro number }}=\dfrac{V}{V_{0}} \times \dfrac{N_{0}}{N_{A}}$
Mass $m$ of given sample volume$=$ Number of moles $\times$ Molar mass
$\Rightarrow m=\dfrac{V}{V_{0}} \times \dfrac{N_{0}}{N_{A}} \times M$
So,the correct option is (3).

question: Match the following concepts related to the molecular nature of matter and choose the correct option from the codes given below.
explanation:
A corresponds to Q as the Atomic Hypothesis is about matter being made of atoms, which are considered indivisible and infinitesimal parts.
B corresponds to P because Avogadro's Law states that equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure have the same number of molecules.
C corresponds to S because the kinetic energy of molecules is directly related to the temperature of the gas, according to the kinetic theory of gases.
D corresponds to R due to the Law of Equipartition of Energy, which states that each degree of freedom in a system in thermal equilibrium has an average energy associated with it that is proportional to the absolute temperature of the system. However, Boyle's Law, which states that volume is inversely proportional to pressure at constant temperature, is related to option R. Correct option is (4).

options.text: A - P, B - Q, C - R, D - S
A - Q,     B - P,  C - S,  D - R
A - R,     B - S,  C - P,  D - Q

question:
As per kinetic theory of gases which of the following statement $(s)$ is/are true?
I. Temperature of a gas is a measure of average kinetic energy of a molecule.
II. Temperature of a gas depends on the nature of the gas.
III. Heavier molecule has lower average speed.
IV. Lighter molecule has lower average speed.
explanation:
As per kinetic theory of gases,
Statement I Since, average kinetic energy of gases,
$K E_{a v}=\dfrac{3}{2} K T$
$\therefore K E_{a v} \propto T$
Hence, temperature of gases is a measure of average kinetic energy of a molecule is correct.
So, option (1) is the correct.
Statement II As temperature of gas is independent of nature of gas.
Hence, option (2) is the incorrect.
Statement III Since, $v_{r m s}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3 R T}{M}}$
$\because \quad v_{r m s} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$
As $M$ increases $v_{r m s}$ decreases.
Hence, option (3) is the correct.

question: A molecule is travelling in air at $300 K$ and 1 atm , and the radius of the molecule is $0.6 \times 10^{-10} {~m}$. Calculate the approx. mean free path of the molecule. (The number density is $2.44 \times 10^{25}$ molecules $/ {m}^{3}$)
explanation:
As, mean free path,
$\lambda=\dfrac{K T}{\sqrt{2} \pi d^{2} p}$
$\lambda=\dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.414 \pi \times\left(1.2 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2} \times 1.01 \times 10^{5}}$
$=\dfrac{0.2}{\pi} \times 10^{-5} {~m}$

question: When some amount of heat energy is supplied to a monatomic gas, the percentage of heat energy used for increasing the internal energy of the gas  $(\gamma  = 5/3)$  is
explanation:
The heat $Q$ is converted into the internal energy and work.
According to the first law of thermodynamics,
$\Delta Q=\Delta U+\Delta W$
$n c_{P} d T=n c_{v} d T+W$
The fraction of heat converted into internal energy
$\dfrac{\Delta U}{\Delta Q}=\dfrac{\Delta Q-\Delta W}{\Delta Q}$
$=\dfrac{n c_{P} d T-\left(n c_{P} d T-n c_{V} d T\right)}{n c_{P} d T}$
$=\dfrac{n c_{v} d T}{n c_{P} d T}=\dfrac{c_{V}}{c_{P}}=\dfrac{3}{5} \quad\left(\because r=\dfrac{5}{3}\right)$
Therefore percentage of heat energy used$=\dfrac{3}{5} \times 100 \%=60 \%$

question: A cylinder consists of a gas mixture of helium and oxygen. If the mass of helium is $4\,g$ and the mass of oxygen is $32\,g$, then the ratio of specific heat of the mixture $\left(C_{p} / C_{V}\right)$ is
explanation:
Helium is a monoatomic gas.
$\therefore$ Degrees of freedom, $f_{1}=3$
Oxygen is a diatomic gas.
$\therefore$ Degrees of freedom, $f_{2}=5$
$g$ helium contains $N_{1}=N_{A}=6.023 \times 10^{23}$
number of molecules
$32 g$ oxygen contains $N_{2}=N_{A}$ number of molecules.
(As atomic mass of oxygen is $16\,g$)
$\therefore \dfrac{C_{P}}{C_{V}}=\gamma=\dfrac{N_{1}\left(2+f_{1}\right)+N_{2}\left(2+f_{2}\right)}{N_{1} f_{1}+N_{2} f_{2}}$
$=\dfrac{N_{A}(2+3)+N_{A}(2+5)}{3 N_{A}+5 N_{A}}$
$=\dfrac{5+7}{3+5}=\dfrac{12}{8}=\dfrac{3}{2}$

question: An ideal gas has molar heat capacity $C_{V}$ at constant volume. The gas undergoes a process in which the temperature changes as $T=T_{0}\left(1+\alpha V^{2}\right)$, where, $T$ and $V$ are the temperature and volume respectively, $T_{0}$ and $\alpha$ are positive constants. The molar heat capacity $C$ of the gas is given as $C=C_{V}+R f(V)$, where, $f(V)$ is a function of volume. The expression for molar heat capacity ' $C$ ' is
explanation:
Given. $T=T_{0}\left(V+\alpha V^{2}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{dT}}{{dV}} = {T_0} \cdot 2\alpha V \Rightarrow dV = \frac{{dT}}{{{T_0} \cdot 2\alpha V}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
From first law of thermodynamics,
$d Q=d U+d W \Rightarrow n C \cdot d T=n C_{V} \cdot d T+p d V$
$\Rightarrow n C \cdot d T+n C_{V} \cdot d T+p\left(\dfrac{d T}{T_{0} \cdot 2 \alpha V}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow C = {C_V} + \left( {\frac{p}{n}} \right)\left( {\frac{1}{{{T_0}2\alpha V}}} \right){\text{ }}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Now as gas is ideal.
$p V=n R T$ or $\dfrac{p}{n}=\dfrac{R T}{V}=\dfrac{R\left[T_{0}\left(1+\alpha V^{2}\right)\right]}{V}$
Substituting in Eq. (2), we get,
$C=C_{V}+R \cdot\left(\dfrac{1+\alpha V^{2}}{2 \alpha V^{2}}\right)$

explanation:

Now,
$C_{p}=C_{V}+R$
$C_{p}=(3+f) R+R \Rightarrow C_{p}=(4+f) R$
Now, ratio of $C_{p}$ and $C_{V}$
$\dfrac{C_{p}}{C_{v}}=\dfrac{4+f}{3+f}$

explanation:

${\Rightarrow C_{P}=\dfrac{R}{2 V}+C_{V}}$

question:
${C_P}$ and ${C_V}$ are specific heats at constant pressure and constant volume respectively. It is observed that
${C_P} - {C_V} = $ a for helium gas
$C_{P}-C_{V}=b$ for oxygen gas
Such that, $b$ is $n$ times the $a$. Find out value of $n$.
explanation:
Let molar heat capacity at constant pressure $ = {S_P}$
and molar heat capacity at constant volume $ = {s_V}$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,{s_P} - {s_V} = R$
Now, principal specific heat, $C = \frac{s}{M}$
$\therefore \quad {C_P} - {C_V} = \frac{R}{M}$
$\therefore $ For $He,a = \frac{R}{4};$ for ${O_2},\,b = \frac{R}{{32}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{b}{a} = \frac{R}{{32}} \times \frac{4}{R} = \frac{1}{8}$
$ \Rightarrow b = 0.125a = 0.13a$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm{(Rounding}}\,{\rm{off}}\,{\rm{to}}\,{\rm{two}}\,{\rm{digits)}}$

question:
Assertion: The molecule of a monoatomic gas has three degrees of freedom at room temperature.


Reason: The molecule of a diatomic gas has five degree of freedom.
explanation: A monoatomic gas molecule (like $\mathrm{He}$ ) consists of single atom. It has translational motion in any direction in space. Thus, it has 3 translational degrees of freedom. The molecules of a diatomic gas (like $\left.\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2}\right)$ cannot only translate but also rorate about any one of the three co-ordinate axes. Hence, it can have two rotational degrees of freedom. Thus, a diatomic molecule has 5 degrees of freedom: 3 translational and 2 rotational.

Assertion: Equal masses of helium and oxygen gases are given equal quantities of heat. There will be a greater rise in the temperature of helium compared to that of oxygen.


Reason: The molecular weight of oxygen is more than the molecular weight of helium.
explanation: Helium is a monatomic gas, while oxygen is diatomic. Therefore, the heat given to helium will be totally used up in increasing the translational kinetic energy of its molecules; whereas the heat given to oxygen will be used up in increasing the translational kinetic energy of the molecule and also in increasing the kinetic energy of rotation and vibration. Hence temperature rise of helium is more. Option (2) is correct.

question: $C_{P}$ and $C_{V}$ are specific heats at constant pressure and constant volume respectively. It is observed that $C_{p}-C_{\mathrm{v}}=a$ for hydrogen gas $C_{p}-C_{\mathrm{v}}=b$ for nitrogen gas. The correct relation between $a$ and $\mathrm{b}$ :
explanation:
Molar specific heat $C^{\prime}=\dfrac{\Delta Q}{n \Delta T}$
Specific heat $C=\dfrac{\Delta Q}{m \Delta T}$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{C^{\prime}}{C}=\dfrac{m}{n}=M(\text { Molecular weight }) \\& C=\dfrac{C^{\prime}}{M} \\& C_{P}^{\prime}-C_{V}^{\prime}=R \\& M C_{P}-M C_{V}=R \\& C_{P}-C_{V}=\dfrac{R}{M}\end{aligned}$
For hydrogen $M = 2$
$C_{P}-C_{V}=\dfrac{R}{2}=a$
For nitrogen $M = 28$
$\begin{aligned}& \Rightarrow C_{P}-C_{V}=\dfrac{R}{28}=b \\& \dfrac{a}{b}=14\end{aligned}$

question: The value of the gas constant $(R)$ calculated from the perfect gas equation is 8.32 joules $/ \mathrm{g}$ mole $K$, whereas its value calculated from the knowledge of $C_{P}$ and $C_{V}$ of the gas is $1.98{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} cal/g$ mole of $K$. From this data, the value of $J$ is
explanation:
We know that $C_{P}-C_{V}=\dfrac{R}{J}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad J = \frac{R}{{{C_P} - {C_V}}}{\rm{ }}$
${C_P} - {C_V} = 1.98\frac{{cal}}{{g - mol - K}}$
$R=8.32 \dfrac{J}{g-m o l-K}$
$\therefore J = \frac{{8.32}}{{1.98}} = 4.20\,\,J/cal$

question: A gaseous mixture enclosed in a vessel consists of one mole of a gas $A$ with $(\gamma=5 / 3)$ and another $B$ with $(\gamma=7 / 5)$ at a temperature $T$. The gases $A$ and $B$do not react with each other and assumed to be ideal. Find the number of moles of the gas $B$, if $\gamma$ for the gaseous mixture is $(19 / 13)$.
explanation:
We define specific heat capacity at constant volume
$C_{V}=\dfrac{R}{(\gamma-1)}$
For gas $A,$ $\left(C_{V}\right)_{A}=\dfrac{R}{(5 / 3)-1}=\dfrac{3}{2} R$
For gas $B,{\left( {{C_V}} \right)_B} = \frac{R}{{(7/5) - 1}} = \frac{5}{2}R$
For gaseous mixture,
$\left(C_{V}\right)_{\text {mix }}=\dfrac{R}{(19 / 13)-1}=\dfrac{13}{6} R$
We define specific heat capacity of mixture
$\begin{aligned}& \left(C_{V}\right)_{\text {mix }}=\dfrac{n_{1}\left(C_{V}\right)_{1}+n_{2}\left(C_{V}\right)_{2}}{n_{1}+n_{2}} \\& \dfrac{13}{6} R=\dfrac{1 \times \dfrac{3}{2} R+n \times \dfrac{5}{2} R}{1+n}=\dfrac{(3+5 n)}{2(1+n)}\end{aligned}$
or $13+13 n=9+15 n$, i.e., $n=2$ mole.

question: $4.0 \,g$ of gas occupies 22.4 litres at NTP. The specific heat capacity of the gas at constant volume is $5.0J{\text{ }}{K^{ - 1}}\,mo{l^{ - 1}}$. If the speed of sound in this gas at NTP is $999 m{s^{ - 1}}$ then the specific heat capacity at constant pressure in $J{k^{ - 1}} mo{l^{ - 1}}$ is (Take gas $R = 8.3\,J{\text{ }}{K^{ - 1}} mo{l^{ - 1}})$
explanation:
As the given conditions are at NTP $m = M = 4.0 \,\,g$
(As it occupies 22.4 liters)
${v_{rms}} = 999\,\,m/s\quad R = 8.3J \cdot {k^{ - 1}}mo{l^{ - 1}}$
${v_{rms}} = \sqrt {\frac{{\gamma RT}}{M}} \Rightarrow \gamma = \frac{{M{v^2}}}{{RT}} = 1.6$
$\gamma = \frac{{{C_P}}}{{{C_V}}} \Rightarrow {C_P} = \gamma {C_V} = 1.6 \times 5 = 8J.{k^{ - 1}} mo{l^{ - 1}}$
